specific_decision: School attendance and engagement, Engagement in care planning, Medication adherence, Family time arrangements, Substance misuse decisions, Health appointments (dental, optical, EKG, blood tests), Lifestyle choices (diet, exercise), Emotional wellbeing, Overnight stays at mum's home, Declining advocate services, Declining smoking cessation support
steps_taken: Health appointments (dental, optical, EKG, blood tests)
reasons_for_conclusion: Logan is 15 years old and under a Section 20 care order, meaning her Mum retains full parental responsibility. The care team actively supports Logan's participation in decision-making and encourages her to make informed choices, particularly regarding education. However, final decisions on certain matters are made in Logan's best interests, reflecting her age and legal status. Specific decisions are assessed individually for her capacity to understand and retain information, and any associated risks.

specific_decision: Consent to Accommodation and Care, Managing Medication, Use of Bed Rails
reasons_for_conclusion: Lacks capacity due to moderate dementia affecting understanding and retention. Unable to understand purpose, timing, or risks of medication for decision-specific assessments.

specific_decision: Emotional wellbeing and therapy choices, Substance misuse engagement with services, Medical treatment decisions (e.g., blood tests, medication adherence), Educational engagement and choices, Family contact and relationships, General care planning
steps_taken: Medical treatment decisions (e.g., blood tests, medication adherence)
reasons_for_conclusion: Logan demonstrates capacity in many areas, with her decisions generally respected. The care team provides extensive support, education, and incentives to guide her towards choices that promote her well-being, while implementing necessary restrictions for safety or legal compliance. There are no mentions of formal 'best interest' decisions being made for her without her involvement, reinforcing an assumption of her capacity to make her own decisions, with support.

assessor: Emma Williams with Dr. James Park (Psychiatrist)
reason_for_assessment: Danny wants more independence with money. Family concerned about financial exploitation risk

diagnostic_test.impairment_details: Moderate learning disability and autism spectrum disorder affecting executive function and abstract reasoning

understand_information.evidence: With visual aids and concrete examples, Danny could understand concepts of income, expenses, and saving. Understood need to pay rent and bills

retain_information.evidence: Danny could recall information about budgeting when asked 15 minutes later. Could explain what rent means and why bills need paying

weigh_information.evidence: With structured support, Danny demonstrated ability to weigh up simple choices (e.g., buying game vs saving for bigger item). Needed support with more complex decisions

communicate_decision.evidence: Danny clearly communicated his wishes regarding money management

steps_taken: Broke down information into small steps
timing_considerations: Danny's capacity is consistent throughout day. Better when not anxious or overwhelmed
reasons_for_conclusion: Danny has capacity to make day-to-day financial decisions with appropriate support systems in place. He understands concepts of budgeting, can retain and weigh up information about routine financial choices, and can communicate his decisions. Assessment concluded he has capacity for everyday financial management within a supported framework. However, lacks capacity for complex financial decisions (investments, large purchases) which would require deputy involvement

best_interests_decision.reasons: Not required - Danny has capacity for this decision

people_involved.name: Danny Foster
people_involved.views: Wants to manage own money but happy to have help with complicated things

people_involved.name: Parents - Mr & Mrs Foster
people_involved.views: Supportive of Danny having more independence. Agreed to supported budgeting system

people_involved.name: Emma Williams
people_involved.views: Danny has shown he can manage daily budget with support framework. Recommend weekly budgeting sessions

assessor: Dr. Helen Roberts (GP) with Sarah Johnson (Care Manager)
specific_decision: Consent to remain in care home and acceptance of personal care
reason_for_assessment: Client has dementia and family questioned capacity to consent to care

diagnostic_test.impairment_details: Diagnosed Alzheimer's disease (moderate stage). Short-term memory significantly impaired. Some confusion about time and place

understand_information.evidence: Betty was able to understand that she lives in a care home and why. Understood that staff help her with washing and dressing. Could repeat back this information immediately after being told

retain_information.evidence: After 5 minute conversation about something else, Betty could not recall the discussion about care. This was repeated 3 times with same result

weigh_information.evidence: Due to inability to retain information, Betty cannot weigh up the pros and cons of accepting care. Cannot recall alternatives discussed

communicate_decision.evidence: Betty can clearly communicate her immediate wishes and feelings about care

steps_taken: Assessment conducted during morning when Betty is most alert
timing_considerations: Betty's capacity is better in the morning. Assessment conducted at 10am when she is most settled and alert
reasons_for_conclusion: While Betty can understand information about her care in the moment, she is unable to retain this information for long enough to weigh it up and make an informed decision. This inability is due to her Alzheimer's disease. She fails stages 2 and 3 of the functional test. Assessment conducted in accordance with MCA 2005

best_interests_decision.persons_wishes: Betty expresses she likes living at the home, likes the staff, and wants to 'stay here and be looked after'
best_interests_decision.persons_beliefs: Was a very independent person who valued cleanliness and taking pride in appearance. Would not want to be neglected
best_interests_decision.persons_values: Family very important. Faith important. Dignity and respect paramount
best_interests_decision.decision_made: It is in Betty's best interests to remain in residential care and receive personal care support from staff
best_interests_decision.decision_maker: Margaret Thompson (LPA) in consultation with care team and GP
best_interests_decision.reasons: Betty is safe, well-cared for, and appears content. She has expressed she wants to stay. She cannot safely manage at home even with domiciliary support. Her values of cleanliness and dignity are being upheld. Decision is least restrictive option

people_involved.name: Margaret Thompson
people_involved.views: Wants mother to remain in care home where she is safe and happy. Very supportive of care team

people_involved.name: Dr. Helen Roberts
people_involved.views: Agrees Betty lacks capacity for this decision. Supports residential care as best option
